question.text: Scenario: Mr. Harish Gupta, aged 50, is a business owner with a net worth of Rs. 8 crore. He has Rs. 1 crore available for equity investment and is evaluating two options: Option A — Equity Mutual Fund (Direct Plan, Growth): Large & Mid-Cap Fund with expense ratio 0.75%, SEBI-mandated diversification (minimum 35% large-cap, 35% mid-cap), no minimum investment, exit load 1% if redeemed within 1 year, daily NAV disclosure, SEBI Mutual Fund Regulations apply. Option B — Discretionary Equity PMS: Minimum investment Rs. 50 lakh, fixed management fee 1.5% p.a., performance fee 20% of profits above 10% hurdle rate (no catch-up, high watermark applies), portfolio of 15-25 stocks customized to Harish's risk profile, Rs. 5 crore net worth of PMS provider, quarterly portfolio disclosure. Additional information: Harish is in the 30% income tax bracket. He expects a 5-year investment horizon. He wants concentrated, high-conviction stock picking rather than broad diversification. He has a separate emergency fund and does not need liquidity from this investment. Question 5 of 5: Given Harish's profile (Rs. 1 crore, 5-year horizon, wants concentrated stock picking, has emergency fund separately), which option would an investment adviser most appropriately recommend?

answer.text: Only Mutual Fund — PMS is too expensive for any investor
Only PMS — all HNIs should use PMS instead of mutual funds

PMS is more suitable given his preference for concentration and customization, ability to bear higher fees, and HNI status — but the higher cost must be justified by demonstrably better after-fee performance
Neither — he should invest directly in stocks without intermediary

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Harish meets PMS minimum (Rs. 50L), prefers concentrated high-conviction stock picking (MF can't deliver this due to diversification mandates), has a 5-year horizon, and doesn't need liquidity. PMS suits his needs better. However, the adviser must ensure the PMS can deliver alpha after its higher fees (3%+ vs 0.75%). **The Trap:** PMS isn't automatically better for all HNIs — the recommendation depends on the client's specific needs. An HNI wanting broad diversification may be better served by mutual funds. **Cross-Exam Context:** MF vs PMS suitability analysis is a key advisory skill tested in X-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The acid test for PMS recommendation: can the PMS generate at least 2-3% excess return over comparable MFs to justify its higher fees? If not, the mutual fund is the better choice.
